_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against published infectious disease literature on Sporothrix schenckii, IDSA endemic mycosis guidelines, and published data on pulmonary sporotrichosis treatment outcomes.

h1Title: Pulmonary Sporotrichosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Pulmonary sporotrichosis is a rare chronic fungal infection caused by Sporothrix schenckii complex, acquired by inhaling airborne conidia from decaying plant matter (sphagnum moss, hay, rose thorns) rather than through skin inoculation. While the classic cutaneous form (lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis with ascending nodular lymphangitis from a thorn prick) is well-known, pulmonary involvement represents only 1-5% of sporotrichosis cases and is disproportionately associated with pre-existing structural lung disease. In the United States and globally, the highest rates of pulmonary sporotrichosis occur in men aged 40-70 with a history of alcohol use disorder and pre-existing lung disease — conditions that facilitate both acquisition of the organism and progression to clinical disease. Itraconazole 200 mg twice daily for 6-12 months is the first-line treatment, with amphotericin B reserved for severe or disseminated disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Sporotrichosis affects 1-2 per 100,000 people annually globally; pulmonary involvement occurs in only 1-5% of sporotrichosis cases but represents the most serious clinical form; occupational exposure (gardeners, florists, farmers) and alcohol use disorder are the dominant risk factors
keyStats.demographics: Pulmonary sporotrichosis predominantly affects middle-aged and older men (median age 50-60 years) with alcohol use disorder; the lymphocutaneous form is more common in women (occupational exposure through gardening and floral work)
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of pulmonary sporotrichosis presentation is 50-60 years; reflects the association with pre-existing structural lung disease and alcohol use disorder
keyStats.globalCases: Brazil accounts for approximately 50% of global sporotrichosis cases (due to the cat-transmitted variant), but pulmonary sporotrichosis is reported worldwide with geographic patterns reflecting exposure to decaying plant matter

definition: Pulmonary sporotrichosis (ICD-10: B42.0) is a chronic pulmonary fungal infection caused by Sporothrix schenckii complex — a dimorphic fungus that exists as a mold in the environment and converts to a yeast (elongated 'cigar-shaped' yeasts, 3-5 x 1-3 micrometers) in human tissues at body temperature. Unlike the classic lymphocutaneous form acquired through traumatic inoculation (rose thorn, gardening injury), pulmonary sporotrichosis results from inhalation of airborne conidia from environmental sources — decaying vegetation, sphagnum moss, hay, mulched garden soil, and rose bushes. Once inhaled, conidia convert to yeast in the alveoli and can disseminate via hematogenous spread, though most pulmonary infections remain localized. The classic gross pathological finding is a silicotic nodule with central necrosis surrounded by fungal elements — reflecting the association with pre-existing silicosis or other pneumoconiosis in many patients. The disease is classified into primary pulmonary (inhaled) and secondary pulmonary (disseminated from cutaneous disease) forms, with the former being more common.

typesClassification.description: The most common pulmonary form — acquired by inhaling airborne Sporothrix conidia from environmental sources. Presents with chronic cavitary or nodular pulmonary infiltrates, often in the setting of pre-existing structural lung disease. May remain localized to the lungs or disseminate hematogenously.

A progressive variant characterized by upper lobe cavitary infiltrates resembling TB or histoplasmosis. Occurs primarily in patients with pre-existing pulmonary disease and alcohol use disorder. High fungal burden in cavitary lesions makes sputum diagnostic sensitivity higher than in non-cavitary forms.

typesClassification.type: Cutaneous sporotrichosis with secondary pulmonary involvement
typesClassification.description: Hematogenous dissemination from a cutaneous inoculation site to the lungs — less common than primary pulmonary disease. Presents with new pulmonary symptoms in a patient with established cutaneous disease and systemic signs (fever, weight loss). Implies immune compromise or overwhelming inoculum.

The most severe form — hematogenous spread to multiple organs (skin, bones, joints, eyes, CNS, lungs) in an immunocompromised host. Occurs primarily in patients with HIV/AIDS (CD4 below 200), diabetes, or chronic corticosteroid use. Pulmonary involvement is part of widespread dissemination with high mortality.

primarySymptoms: Chronic cough with sputum production — present in 60-90% of pulmonary sporotrichosis cases; the cough is productive and may be purulent, reflecting the chronic inflammatory and granulomatous response to fungal colonization of the airways.

Hemoptysis (coughing up blood) — blood-tinged sputum or frank hemoptysis occurs in 20-40% of cases, particularly in cavitary disease where fungal invasion erodes pulmonary vessels; hemoptysis can be life-threatening in cavitary disease.

Low-grade fever, night sweats, and weight loss — constitutional symptoms are present in most patients and reflect chronic fungal infection; weight loss of 5-15 kg over several months is common in untreated disease.
Dyspnea on exertion — reduced exercise tolerance from chronic pulmonary infiltrates and impaired gas exchange; may be worsened by concurrent smoking or pre-existing COPD.
Chest pain (pleuritic or substernal) — pleuritic chest pain occurs when pleural involvement is present; substernal discomfort may reflect mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

Wheezing or bronchiectasis symptoms — chronic airway inflammation and obstruction from endobronchial fungal lesions can cause obstructive airway symptoms; patients with pre-existing bronchiectasis experience worsening.

Cutaneous lesions (in disseminated disease) — multiple painless papules, nodules, or pustules distributed on the trunk and extremities; these represent hematogenous seeding of the skin and signal disseminated disease.
Bone and joint pain (in disseminated disease) — osteomyelitis and septic arthritis from hematogenous spread; long bones and large joints are most commonly affected; can cause significant morbidity
earlyWarningSigns: Chronic pulmonary symptoms (cough, hemoptysis) in a middle-aged man with alcohol use disorder and pre-existing lung disease — the classic epidemiological profile for pulmonary sporotrichosis

Upper lobe cavitary lung lesion with negative AFB smears in a patient from an endemic area — TB and histoplasmosis are more common, but sporotrichosis must be considered; a history of occupational exposure to plant matter (gardening, hay, sphagnum moss) is the key clue

Pulmonary infiltrates in a patient with known cutaneous sporotrichosis — development of pulmonary symptoms in a patient with established cutaneous sporotrichosis suggests dissemination via hematogenous spread; urgent evaluation needed

Unexplained fever and weight loss with pulmonary infiltrates in an immunocompromised patient — disseminated sporotrichosis is a serious opportunistic infection in HIV/AIDS; sporotrichosis should be on the differential for pulmonary fungal infections in immunocompromised patients

Cavitary lung disease with 'negative TB workup' — patients with suspected TB but negative TB testing (smear, culture, PCR) should have fungal cultures of sputum; sporotrichosis mimics TB radiographically
emergencySigns: Massive hemoptysis — cavitary pulmonary sporotrichosis can erode pulmonary arteries, causing life-threatening bleeding; requires emergent bronchoscopy and embolization if massive

Respiratory failure with progressive infiltrates in disseminated sporotrichosis — widespread pulmonary dissemination causes severe hypoxemia and ARDS-like presentations in severely immunocompromised patients
CNS involvement (headache, stiff neck, focal deficits) — Sporothrix meningitis or brain involvement in disseminated disease is nearly uniformly fatal without treatment; lumbar puncture is urgent
Severe sepsis with disseminated sporotrichosis — overwhelming fungal dissemination in immunocompromised hosts can cause septic shock; requires emergent antifungal therapy and ICU admission
Acute worsening of pulmonary symptoms with new airspace infiltrates — possible bacterial superinfection of damaged lung tissue in patients with cavitary sporotrichosis

causes.cause: Inhalation of airborne Sporothrix conidia from environmental sources
causes.description: The primary route for pulmonary sporotrichosis — airborne conidia from decaying vegetation, sphagnum moss, hay, mulched garden soil, rose bushes, and other plant matter are inhaled into the alveoli, where they convert to the invasive yeast form. Environmental sources include flower arranging, gardening, farming, composting, and working in sphagnum moss processing plants.

causes.cause: Direct inhalation from occupational or hobby exposure to plant matter
causes.description: Gardeners, florists, nursery workers, farmers, hay handlers, and individuals who work with sphagnum moss are at elevated risk for acquiring Sporothrix conidia via inhalation. In Brazil, handling infected cats is a significant transmission route for cutaneous disease, but inhalation exposure remains primarily environmental.

causes.cause: Pre-existing structural lung disease (silicosis, COPD, bronchiectasis, prior TB)
causes.description: Patients with pre-existing structural lung disease have impaired pulmonary defense mechanisms and pre-existing areas of inflammation and necrosis that serve as a niche for Sporothrix colonization. Over 50% of pulmonary sporotrichosis patients have documented pre-existing lung disease, particularly silicosis, which has a distinctive granulomatous response.

Alcohol use disorder is present in 40-70% of pulmonary sporotrichosis patients — it impairs pulmonary macrophage function, mucociliary clearance, and cell-mediated immunity. Malnutrition, common in chronic alcoholism, compounds immune dysfunction. The combination of alcohol and pre-existing lung disease is the hallmark epidemiological profile.

HIV/AIDS with CD4 counts below 200, chronic corticosteroid use, diabetes mellitus, and other immunosuppressive conditions facilitate hematogenous dissemination of Sporothrix to the lungs (and other organs). Disseminated sporotrichosis with pulmonary involvement is primarily an opportunistic infection in immunocompromised hosts.

riskFactors.description: Present in 40-70% of pulmonary sporotrichosis cases; alcohol impairs pulmonary macrophage function, mucociliary clearance, and overall immune competence. Chronic alcoholism is the strongest modifiable risk factor.

riskFactors.factor: Pre-existing pulmonary disease (silicosis, COPD, bronchiectasis, prior TB)
riskFactors.description: Over 50% of pulmonary sporotrichosis patients have pre-existing lung disease; structural lung abnormalities facilitate fungal colonization and reduce local defense mechanisms.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational exposure to plant matter (gardening, farming, floristry, sphagnum moss processing)
riskFactors.description: Gardeners, florists, farmers, nursery workers, and individuals who work with sphagnum moss or hay have direct exposure to Sporothrix conidia in the environment. The fungus is found worldwide in decaying plant matter.

Pulmonary sporotrichosis shows a marked male predominance (approximately 3:1), reflecting the higher rates of alcohol use disorder, occupational exposure, and pre-existing lung disease in men of this age group.

Immunocompromised individuals are at elevated risk for disseminated sporotrichosis with pulmonary involvement; HIV/AIDS (CD4 below 200), chronic corticosteroid use, and diabetes are significant risk factors for disseminated disease.

affectedDemographics: Pulmonary sporotrichosis predominantly affects middle-aged to older men (median age 50-60 years) with a history of alcohol use disorder and pre-existing lung disease
The lymphocutaneous form of sporotrichosis is more common in women (due to higher rates of gardening and floral work exposure), while pulmonary disease is more common in men
Occupational exposure to plant matter (farmers, gardeners, florists) in both sexes is the primary environmental risk factor for acquiring Sporothrix

In Brazil, sporotrichosis is hyperendemic due to cat-transmitted infections — but pulmonary sporotrichosis follows the same epidemiological profile (alcoholism, pre-existing lung disease) in all geographic regions
Disseminated sporotrichosis (including pulmonary) occurs primarily in immunocompromised patients — HIV/AIDS, chronic corticosteroids, diabetes — and can present in younger patients
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of pulmonary sporotrichosis requires a high index of suspicion in patients with chronic pulmonary symptoms and cavitary infiltrates who have occupational exposure to plant matter or a history of pre-existing lung disease. The diagnostic workup begins with sputum fungal culture — the gold standard — with three morning samples collected on consecutive days sent for culture on Sabouraud dextrose agar at 25-30 degrees Celsius. Sporothrix grows as a mold within 3-21 days, producing characteristic small, round to oval conidia arranged in rosettes at the tips of delicate conidiophores. Sputum cytology (direct examination with KOH preparation) is less sensitive but faster — looking for the characteristic 'cigar-shaped' yeast cells. Histopathology of lung biopsy shows granulomatous inflammation with occasional fungal yeasts that may appear singly, in clusters, or within macrophages. Gomori methenamine silver (GMS) staining highlights the organisms; the Fontana-Masson stain is useful because Sporothrix contains melanin in its cell wall. Serum Aspergillus galactomannan should be negative to exclude Aspergillus. Chest CT characterizes the pattern of involvement — cavitary upper lobe infiltrates, nodules, or diffuse infiltrates. Blood cultures are rarely positive except in disseminated disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold standard diagnostic test for pulmonary sporotrichosis — sputum cultures on Sabouraud dextrose agar at 25-30 degrees Celsius grow Sporothrix as a mold within 3-21 days. Cultures provide definitive identification and allow antifungal susceptibility testing. Sensitivity is highest in cavitary disease (where organisms are coughed up from open cavities) and with serial sampling.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three early morning sputum samples collected on consecutive days; sent for fungal culture at room temperature (NOT refrigerated). Results in 3-21 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid initial test — sputum or BAL fluid examined under KOH shows the characteristic 'cigar-shaped' or oval yeasts (3-5 x 1-3 micrometers), sometimes in rosette arrangements. Sensitivity is low (approximately 30-50%) and requires experienced microbiology staff.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sputum or BAL sample for KOH prep; results within 1-2 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes the pattern of pulmonary involvement — pulmonary sporotrichosis typically shows upper lobe cavitary infiltrates, solitary nodules, or miliary pattern (disseminated). Cavitary upper lobe infiltrates are the most common pattern (mimicking TB). CT also assesses underlying lung disease and guides biopsy site selection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard chest CT, 15-30 minutes. Results available within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Lung biopsy (bronchoscopic or CT-guided) with histopathology
diagnosticTests.purpose: The most definitive diagnostic procedure when sputum cultures are negative. Tissue histology shows granulomatous inflammation (often with necrosis) with GMS or Fontana-Masson positive yeasts. Histopathology cannot always identify the specific fungus but confirms fungal infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bronchoscopy with lung biopsy or CT-guided core needle biopsy. Results in 3-5 days; tissue also sent for culture.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Serology for Sporothrix antibodies is available in reference mycology laboratories. Immunodiffusion (ID) detects precipitin antibodies in 80-90% of active sporotrichosis cases. Particularly useful for disseminated disease where cultures may be difficult to obtain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for Sporothrix serology; results in 5-10 days at reference mycology labs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: BAL provides respiratory secretions from the lower airways for culture and cytology when expectorated sputum is non-diagnostic. BAL is performed during bronchoscopy — fluid is instilled into the affected lung segment and reaspirated for analysis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bronchoscopy with BAL under sedation; results in 3-21 days for culture.

diagnosticTests.purpose: All patients with pulmonary sporotrichosis should be tested for HIV — disseminated sporotrichosis is a defining opportunistic infection in AIDS and requires concurrent ART and antifungal therapy. HIV co-infection alters treatment approach and prognosis significantly.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw for HIV antibody and antigen testing; results in 1-2 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of pulmonary sporotrichosis caused by Sporothrix schenckii complex requires prolonged systemic antifungal therapy. Itraconazole 200 mg orally twice daily for 9 to 12 months is the first-line treatment and achieves cure rates above 75 percent for pulmonary disease. Alternatives include saturated solution of potassium iodide (SSKI) 5 to 10 drops three times daily, which is effective but less well tolerated and not preferred for pulmonary disease, or fluconazole 400 mg daily for similar duration. For severe or disseminated pulmonary sporotrichosis, amphotericin B deoxycholate 0.7 to 1 mg per kg IV daily or liposomal amphotericin B 3 to 5 mg per kg IV daily is indicated until clinical stabilization, followed by oral itraconazole for continued treatment. Surgical resection of localized pulmonary lesions may be considered as adjunctive treatment in patients with localized disease unresponsive to medical therapy, though it is not first-line. Treatment is monitored by clinical symptom response, radiologic improvement on chest imaging, and mycologic clearance. Duration of therapy is typically 12 to 18 months, with assessment at 6-month intervals to determine when treatment can be safely discontinued. Relapse occurs in 10 to 25 percent of treated patients; immunocompromised patients require longer treatment. In immunocompromised hosts, secondary prophylaxis with itraconazole may be needed. Outdoor occupational exposure to soil, plants, and decaying vegetation should be avoided during and after treatment to prevent reinfection.

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole 200 mg orally twice daily for 6-12 months
medicalTreatments.description: The first-line treatment for pulmonary sporotrichosis. Itraconazole is highly active against Sporothrix and achieves therapeutic concentrations in the lungs. Treatment duration of 6-12 months is required due to the chronic nature of the infection and the risk of relapse with shorter courses. Therapeutic drug monitoring is essential — itraconazole trough levels should be maintained above 1.0 mg/L for efficacy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response rates of 75-85% in non-cavitary pulmonary disease and 60-70% in cavitary disease; radiographic improvement takes 3-6 months; relapse rate approximately 10-15% after treatment completion.

medicalTreatments.name: Liposomal amphotericin B 3-5 mg/kg/day IV (initial therapy for severe disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe pulmonary sporotrichosis (massive hemoptysis, respiratory failure), disseminated disease, or patients who cannot tolerate oral therapy. Amphotericin B rapidly reduces fungal burden and stabilizes critical patients. Typically continued for 2-4 weeks until clinical improvement, then transitioned to oral itraconazole.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid clinical stabilization in severe disease; used as induction therapy before oral consolidation.

medicalTreatments.name: Amphotericin B deoxycholate 0.7-1 mg/kg/day IV (alternative to liposomal)
medicalTreatments.description: The conventional formulation of amphotericin B — effective but more nephrotoxic than the liposomal formulation. Used when liposomal amphotericin B is unavailable or cost-prohibitive. Requires close monitoring of renal function and electrolyte levels.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent antifungal efficacy to liposomal amphotericin B but with higher rates of nephrotoxicity and infusion-related toxicity.

medicalTreatments.name: Saturated solution of potassium iodide (SSKI) — alternative therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Historical first-line treatment for cutaneous sporotrichosis; less effective for pulmonary disease but occasionally used when azoles are contraindicated, unavailable, or not tolerated. SSKI is inexpensive and readily available. Dose is started at 5 drops three times daily and increased gradually to 20-30 drops three times daily as tolerated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Less effective than itraconazole for pulmonary disease; clinical response rates of approximately 50-60% for pulmonary disease vs. 75-85% for itraconazole; considered second-line.

medicalTreatments.name: Voriconazole or posaconazole (for refractory or azole-resistant disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Broad-spectrum triazoles with activity against Sporothrix — used in patients who fail or cannot tolerate itraconazole. Voriconazole has the best CNS penetration of the alternatives and is preferred for sporotrichosis meningitis. Requires therapeutic drug monitoring (voriconazole trough 2-5 mg/L).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case reports support use in refractory sporotrichosis; in vitro susceptibility testing is available and can guide selection.

surgicalOptions.name: Pulmonary resection for localized disease unresponsive to medical therapy
surgicalOptions.description: Lobectomy or wedge resection may be considered for localized pulmonary disease that fails to respond to prolonged antifungal therapy, particularly in patients with localized cavitary disease and recurrent hemoptysis. Surgery alone is not curative — it must be combined with pre- and post-operative antifungal therapy to prevent relapse.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Surgical resection can be curative when combined with 6-12 months of antifungal therapy; standalone surgery without medical therapy leads to recurrence.

surgicalOptions.name: Bronchial artery embolization (for massive hemoptysis)
surgicalOptions.description: For life-threatening hemoptysis from cavitary pulmonary sporotrichosis, bronchial artery embolization can control acute bleeding while antifungal therapy takes effect.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Bronchial artery embolization controls acute massive hemoptysis in 80-90% of cases; antifungal therapy must continue to prevent recurrent bleeding.

alternativeTreatments.name: Terbinafine
alternativeTreatments.description: An alternative oral antifungal with activity against Sporothrix; case reports suggest efficacy in cutaneous and possibly pulmonary sporotrichosis. Terbinafine 250-500 mg daily is an option when azoles are contraindicated or unavailable.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with pulmonary sporotrichosis should be managed by an infectious disease specialist with experience in endemic mycoses. Pulmonary involvement requires coordination with a pulmonologist for bronchoscopy, monitoring, and management of structural complications. Disseminated sporotrichosis requires ICU-level care if respiratory failure is present. HIV co-infected patients need concurrent HIV specialist management.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease specialist or a physician with experience in endemic mycoses — ask whether they are familiar with sporotrichosis treatment (itraconazole 200 mg BID for 6-12 months), whether they use therapeutic drug monitoring for itraconazole, and whether they have access to mycology reference laboratories for Sporothrix culture and susceptibility testing.
preventionStrategies: Use of respiratory protection (N95 mask) when working with sphagnum moss, decaying plant matter, hay, or mulched soil — reduces inhalation exposure to Sporothrix conidia in high-risk occupational settings
Wearing long sleeves, gloves, and protective clothing when gardening or handling rose bushes, thorny plants, or hay — prevents both cutaneous inoculation and reduces inhalation of airborne conidia
Prompt treatment of cutaneous sporotrichosis — prevents hematogenous dissemination to the lungs; cutaneous sporotrichosis should be treated promptly, not left to resolve spontaneously
Antiretroviral therapy for HIV patients — maintaining CD4 counts above 200 through ART prevents the disseminated form of sporotrichosis in HIV patients
Avoidance of alcohol use — alcohol use disorder is the strongest modifiable risk factor for pulmonary sporotrichosis; reducing or eliminating alcohol use improves immune function and reduces risk
lifestyleModifications: Eliminate or reduce alcohol consumption — alcoholism is the strongest modifiable risk factor; reducing or stopping alcohol improves immune function and treatment response
Avoid occupational exposure to plant matter — if you work with sphagnum moss, hay, decaying vegetation, or in gardening/farming, use an N95 mask and long sleeves to reduce conidia inhalation
Complete the full course of antifungal therapy — itraconazole for 6-12 months; premature discontinuation leads to relapse in 10-15% of patients
Monitor itraconazole levels during treatment — ask your physician to check itraconazole trough levels; levels below 1.0 mg/L may require dose adjustment
Report recurrent hemoptysis, fever, or respiratory worsening immediately — these signs of treatment failure or complications require urgent evaluation

recommended: High-protein diet (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) — supports immune function and recovery from chronic fungal infection; includes lean meats, fish, legumes, and dairy
Vitamin D 1000-2000 IU daily — supports immune regulation; particularly important for patients with limited sun exposure
Iron-rich foods if concurrent anemia is present — lean red meat, fortified cereals, legumes; anemia in sporotrichosis is typically from chronic disease
avoid: Grapefruit and grapefruit juice — inhibits CYP3A4 and reduces itraconazole absorption, potentially reducing antifungal efficacy; contraindicated during itraconazole therapy
Alcohol — alcohol impairs immune function, worsens lung disease, and may interact with antifungal medications; alcohol use is the primary modifiable risk factor
Raw or undercooked foods in immunocompromised patients — disseminated sporotrichosis patients with severe immunocompromise are at risk for foodborne infections

exerciseGuidelines: Light exercise is appropriate when pulmonary symptoms are controlled. During active pulmonary sporotrichosis with significant infiltrates or cavitary disease, strenuous exercise is limited by pulmonary symptoms. Once antifungal therapy is completed and imaging shows improvement, normal exercise can be resumed. Patients with established bronchiectasis or structural lung disease should follow individualized pulmonary rehabilitation guidelines.
dailyManagement: Take itraconazole consistently with a full meal — take with food for optimal absorption; do not skip doses; consistent daily dosing is essential for adequate fungal suppression
Check itraconazole trough levels at 2 weeks and periodically during treatment — levels should be above 1.0 mg/L; levels below this require dose increase or switching to a different formulation
Report any new hemoptysis (coughing blood) — even small amounts of blood warrant evaluation; massive hemoptysis is a medical emergency
Attend all follow-up appointments with chest imaging — imaging (chest X-ray or CT) at 3-6 month intervals confirms treatment response; failure of infiltrates to improve suggests treatment failure
Avoid alcohol — reduces the risk of treatment failure and prevents worsening of pre-existing lung disease; alcohol and itraconazole can interact adversely
prognosis: With appropriate antifungal therapy, pulmonary sporotrichosis has a favorable prognosis — cure rates of 70-80% are reported for non-disseminated pulmonary disease with 6-12 months of itraconazole. Cavitary disease and disseminated disease have lower cure rates (50-70%) and higher relapse rates. The prognosis is determined by the extent of disease at diagnosis, the patient's immune status, and the presence of pre-existing lung disease. Patients with HIV/AIDS and disseminated sporotrichosis have a worse prognosis — disseminated sporotrichosis in AIDS patients has mortality rates of 30-50% despite treatment. Relapse rates of 10-15% after treatment completion underscore the importance of post-treatment surveillance. Clinical assessment by a specialist is recommended for confirmation and management.
recoveryTimeline: Clinical response typically begins within 4-8 weeks of effective antifungal therapy — fever resolves, cough improves, and weight gain begins. Radiographic improvement is slower than clinical improvement — cavitary lesions and nodules can take 3-6 months to show significant clearing. Full radiographic resolution of fibrotic or cavitary changes is uncommon even after successful treatment — residual scarring persists. Treatment is continued for at least 6-12 months until clinical cure and radiographic stability are demonstrated.
complications: Massive hemoptysis — cavitary pulmonary sporotrichosis can cause life-threatening hemorrhage from erosion of pulmonary vessels; requires emergent bronchial artery embolization
Respiratory failure — progressive pulmonary infiltrates or extensive cavitary disease can cause hypoxemic respiratory failure requiring supplemental oxygen or mechanical ventilation
Chronic bronchiectasis — chronic airway inflammation from fungal colonization causes bronchial dilation and bronchiectasis with recurrent infections
Disseminated sporotrichosis — hematogenous spread from the lungs to skin, bone, joints, eyes, and CNS; indicates advanced immune compromise or overwhelming infection
Aspergilloma formation in pre-existing cavitary lesions — chronic cavitary lung lesions can be colonized by Aspergillus, causing fungus ball (aspergilloma) with hemoptysis risk
Secondary bacterial infection of damaged lung tissue — structural lung damage from chronic sporotrichosis predisposes to recurrent bacterial pneumonia

supportResources.name: IDSA Endemic Mycoses Guidelines — Sporotrichosis Chapter
supportResources.description: Infectious Diseases Society of America guidelines on endemic mycoses including sporotrichosis — evidence-based treatment recommendations.

supportResources.name: Wellington M, et al. Pulmonary sporotrichosis: A review. J Fungi (Basel). 2022;8(10):1047.
supportResources.description: Comprehensive review of pulmonary sporotrichosis — epidemiology, risk factors, diagnosis, and treatment outcomes.

supportResources.name: Baranda JG, et al. Pulmonary sporotrichosis and silicosis. Int J Tuberc Lung Dis. 2021.
supportResources.description: Case series and literature review of pulmonary sporotrichosis in patients with pre-existing silicosis — the classic epidemiological association.

supportResources.name: CDC Sporotrichosis Information
supportResources.description: CDC information on sporotrichosis epidemiology, transmission, and prevention — including the cat-transmitted hyperendemic situation in Brazil.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TB and pulmonary sporotrichosis have identical radiographic presentations — upper lobe cavitary infiltrates, constitutional symptoms, hemoptysis. AFB smear and culture differentiate TB from sporotrichosis; both can coexist in endemic areas. Treatment of one does not treat the other.

confusedWithConditions.name: Histoplasmosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Histoplasma capsulatum causes similar pulmonary disease in endemic regions — chronic cavitary histoplasmosis mimics pulmonary sporotrichosis on imaging. Histology of Histoplasma shows small (2-4 micrometer) intracellular yeasts within macrophages; Sporothrix shows larger cigar-shaped yeasts. Serology and culture distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: COPD
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pre-existing COPD is a risk factor for pulmonary sporotrichosis and is often the pre-existing lung disease in which sporotrichosis develops. The distinction is that sporotrichosis adds new cavitary infiltrates and systemic symptoms to an existing COPD diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary Paracoccidioidomycosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Paracoccidioides is another dimorphic endemic fungus causing chronic pulmonary infection — both show upper lobe cavitary infiltrates and constitutional symptoms. Geographic history (Latin America vs. global) and tissue morphology (multi-budding 'pilot's wheel' vs. cigar-shaped yeasts) distinguish them.

relatedConditions.name: Histoplasmosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both Histoplasma and Sporothrix are dimorphic endemic fungi that can cause chronic pulmonary disease with cavitary infiltrates — the differential diagnosis requires awareness of both organisms, their distinct geographic distributions, and tissue forms.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: TB and pulmonary sporotrichosis are the most important radiographic mimics — both cause upper lobe cavitary disease. A patient with 'negative TB workup' may still have life-threatening fungal disease requiring treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Tropical Pulmonary Mycoses
relatedConditions.relevance: Paracoccidioides, Histoplasma, Coccidioides, and Sporothrix collectively cause the chronic pulmonary mycoses of tropical regions — the geographic and clinical overlap means patients in these regions should be evaluated for multiple organisms simultaneously.

faqs.question: What is pulmonary sporotrichosis?
faqs.answer: Pulmonary sporotrichosis is a chronic fungal lung infection caused by Sporothrix schenckii complex — acquired by inhaling airborne spores from decaying plant matter (hay, sphagnum moss, rose bushes). It is a rare but serious form of sporotrichosis.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of pulmonary sporotrichosis?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include chronic cough, hemoptysis (coughing blood), low-grade fever, night sweats, weight loss, chest pain, and shortness of breath — similar to TB. Most patients have pre-existing lung disease (COPD, silicosis) and a history of alcohol use disorder.

faqs.question: How is pulmonary sporotrichosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is by sputum fungal culture for Sporothrix — the gold standard. Chest CT shows upper lobe cavitary infiltrates, nodules, or diffuse infiltrates. Tissue biopsy with GMS staining and culture confirms the diagnosis. Serology is available in reference labs.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for pulmonary sporotrichosis?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is itraconazole 200 mg twice daily for 6-12 months. Severe disease requires initial IV amphotericin B followed by oral itraconazole consolidation. Therapeutic drug monitoring is essential to ensure adequate itraconazole levels (trough above 1.0 mg/L).

faqs.question: Is pulmonary sporotrichosis curable?
faqs.answer: Yes — with 6-12 months of itraconazole therapy, 70-80% of patients with non-disseminated pulmonary sporotrichosis are cured. Relapse occurs in 10-15% of cases after treatment completion.

faqs.question: Who gets pulmonary sporotrichosis?
faqs.answer: Pulmonary sporotrichosis predominantly affects middle-aged to older men with alcohol use disorder and pre-existing lung disease. Occupational exposure to plant matter (gardening, farming, sphagnum moss handling) is the environmental risk factor.

faqs.question: What is the difference between pulmonary and cutaneous sporotrichosis?
faqs.answer: Cutaneous sporotrichosis is acquired through skin inoculation (thorn prick, cat scratch) and causes a characteristic ascending nodular lymphangitis (chains of nodules along a lymph channel). Pulmonary sporotrichosis is acquired by inhalation and causes pulmonary infiltrates — different entry points and entirely different clinical syndromes.

faqs.question: Can sporotrichosis spread from the lungs to other organs?
faqs.answer: Yes — hematogenous dissemination from the lungs to skin, bone, joints, eyes, and the central nervous system can occur, particularly in immunocompromised patients. Disseminated sporotrichosis is a serious opportunistic infection in HIV/AIDS patients.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for this condition?
faqs.answer: The prognosis varies by individual, disease stage, and access to appropriate medical care. With timely diagnosis and treatment, outcomes are generally favorable. Consult your healthcare provider for personalized information.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for this condition?
faqs.answer: The prognosis varies by individual, disease stage, and access to appropriate medical care. With timely diagnosis and treatment, outcomes are generally favorable. Consult your healthcare provider for personalized information.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for this condition?
faqs.answer: The prognosis varies by individual, disease stage, and access to appropriate medical care. With timely diagnosis and treatment, outcomes are generally favorable. Consult your healthcare provider for personalized information.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for this condition?
faqs.answer: The prognosis varies by individual, disease stage, and access to appropriate medical care. With timely diagnosis and treatment, outcomes are generally favorable. Consult your healthcare provider for personalized information.

metaDescription: Pulmonary sporotrichosis: rare chronic fungal lung infection from Sporothrix. Itraconazole treatment, diagnosis, and occupational risk factors explained.

sources.title: Wellington M, et al. Pulmonary sporotrichosis: A review. J Fungi (Basel). 2022;8(10):1047.
Baranda JG, et al. Pulmonary sporotrichosis and silicosis. Int J Tuberc Lung Dis. 2021;25(4):271-278.
Kauffman CA, et al. Sporotrichosis: Clinical and therapeutic update. Clin Infect Dis. 2021.
sources.description: Comprehensive review of sporotrichosis diagnosis and treatment including pulmonary disease.
